coredata.dc:title: Stability of Pd clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, and defective graphene quantum dots, and their reactivity toward oxygen adsorption: A DFT analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Montejo-Alvaro, F.
Rojas-Chávez, H.
Román-Doval, R.
Mtz-Enriquez, A.I.
Cruz-Martínez, H.
Medina, D.I.
coredata.dc:description: A DFT-based study was carried out to investigate the ground-state structures and properties of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters, the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine (C96H24), B-doped (C95H24:B), and defective (C95H24) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and their reactivity of the Pdn (n = 8–10) on GQDs toward oxygen adsorption. The calculated ground-state structures and properties for the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters are consistent with the previous studies. The stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters adsorbed on GQDs presents the following trend: C95H24 > C95H24:B > C96H24, which demonstrates that defective GQDs is promising for its application as a support material in electrocatalysis. In general, the reactivity of the palladium clusters is not significantly modified by being supported on the C96H24, C95H24:B, and C95H24 GQDs. However, in some cases, the reactivity toward oxygen reduction reaction (using an oxygen atom as a catalytic predictor) is slightly improved (e.g., Pd8/C96H24, Pd8/C95H24:B, Pd9/C95H24:B, and Pd10/C95H24 composites).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pd clusters
Reactivity
Adsorption energies
Structures
Properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structures and properties of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters
Stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, and defective GQDs
Oxygen adsorption on the Pdn (n = 8–10) clusters/pristine, B-doped, and defective GQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ground-state structures of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters.
Properties of the ground-state structures of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters: a) average bond lengths (in Å) and b) binding energies per atom (in eV), n = number of atoms in the clusters.

The pristine (C96H24), B-doped (C95H24:B) and defective (C95H24) GQDs models. The gray, yellow and red spheres represent the hydrogen, carbon and boron atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Ground-state structures of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine GQDs (C96H24).
Ground-state structures of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on B-doped GQDs (C95H24:B).
Ground-state structures of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on defective GQDs (C95H24).
Oxygen adsorption sites on Pdn clusters, Pdn/pristine GQDs, Pdn/B-doped GQDs, and Pdn/defective GQDs, n = 8–10.
Oxygen adsorption energy (in eV) on Pdn clusters, Pdn/pristine GQDs, Pdn/B-doped GQDs, and Pdn/defective GQDs, n = 8–10.

head.ce:title: Stability of Pd clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, and defective graphene quantum dots, and their reactivity toward oxygen adsorption: A DFT analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A DFT-based study was carried out to investigate the ground-state structures and properties of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters, the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine (C96H24), B-doped (C95H24:B), and defective (C95H24) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and their reactivity of the Pdn (n = 8–10) on GQDs toward oxygen adsorption. The calculated ground-state structures and properties for the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters are consistent with the previous studies. The stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters adsorbed on GQDs presents the following trend: C95H24 > C95H24:B > C96H24, which demonstrates that defective GQDs is promising for its application as a support material in electrocatalysis. In general, the reactivity of the palladium clusters is not significantly modified by being supported on the C96H24, C95H24:B, and C95H24 GQDs. However, in some cases, the reactivity toward oxygen reduction reaction (using an oxygen atom as a catalytic predictor) is slightly improved (e.g., Pd8/C96H24, Pd8/C95H24:B, Pd9/C95H24:B, and Pd10/C95H24 composites).

Description: Binding energy (stability) of Pd10 on pristine (C96H24) B-doped (C95H24:B), and defective (C95H24) GQDs and oxygen adsorption energy (reactivity) on Pd10, Pd10/C96H24, Pd10/C95H24:B, and Pd10/C95H24.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: High population growth has increased the demand for fossil fuels, which are responsible for greenhouse gas emissions that exacerbate climate change. To diminish the impact of energy production, it has become essential to convert into more environment-friendly fuel sources such as renewable energy [1,2]. In this context, the polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) have attracted considerable attention due to their high energy-conversion efficiency and low pollutant emissions [1,2]. However, one of the challenges of PEMFCs is the sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Thus, it is required the use of nanocatalysts to accelerate the ORR [2,3]. The commercial Pt/C is the most widely used nanocatalyst [1]. However, it is important to emphasize that commercial Pt/C presents challenges related to catalytic activity, stability, and availability [1,2]. In this sense, Pt-based nanocatalysts are extensively studied as substitutes to commercial Pt/C because they have proven to be more efficient compared to Pt/C for the ORR [3,4]. However, the replacement of Pt in the catalysts is highly desirable [5]. Consequently, the development of cost-effective non-Pt catalysts for ORR has increased substantially [5]. For that reason, Pd nanoparticles (NPs) have gained considerable importance as Pt-free nanocatalysts because they have proved to be good candidates for the ORR [6,7]. The use of Pd NPs for the ORR has been studied both theoretically and experimentally. In theoretical studies, density functional theory (DFT) computations on Pd NPs for the ORR have been performed [8–13]. In these studies, O and O2 binding energies were used to predict the catalytic activity toward the ORR [8–13].

To improve the activity, stability, and utilization of Pd NPs for the ORR, materials with high surface area are used as supports for the NPs (e.g., carbon-based materials) [14]. Among carbon materials, the graphene has received an outstanding attention as support material due to its good electrical, mechanical and structural properties, high surface area and high strength [15,16]. Unfortunately, it has been reported that graphene has low chemical reactivity [17]. Therefore, the interaction between NPs and graphene is weak. Thus, various approaches have been used to modify the graphene reactivity such as doping and vacancies [17,18]. In this sense, there are several DFT theoretical studies on the stability and reactivity of NPs supported on doped and defective graphene toward the ORR. For example, the stability of Pt13 NPs supported on monovacancy defective graphene and their reactivity for the O2 was investigated using DFT calculations. It is demonstrated that a monovacancy site in graphene plays a key role as an anchoring point for Pt13 NPs, which improves the catalytic activity toward the interaction with O2 [19]. After that, the mechanisms of the ORR on defective graphene-supported Pt13 NPs were investigated through DFT predictions. Lim et al. reported that the defective graphene support lowers the activation energy for O2 dissociation. They also found that the energy barrier of the rate-limiting step by reducing the stability of HO species [20]. Recently, the DFT computations were used to analyze substrate effect on the catalytic activity of PtNi NPs supported on pristine and N-doped graphene. It is interesting to highlight that according to Gracia-Espino et al., the N-doped graphene is the best substrate to use for ORR [21]. Following this idea, Tian and coworkers investigated the structural and electronic properties of Pt13 NPs on N-doped graphene and their reactivity toward O through DFT calculations. They demonstrated that the N-doped graphene not only stabilizes the Pt clusters, but also enhances their catalytic performance toward ORR [22]. On the other hand, in recent years, the doped and defective graphene have been investigated as metal-free catalysts, since these have shown promising catalytic activities for the ORR [23–26]. Consequently, it can be a good route to produce active and low-cost metal-free catalysts for ORR.

The doped and defective graphene have proven to be good NPs support materials, since these improve the stability and catalytic activity of the NPs for the ORR [19–22]. Therefore, it is fundamental to investigate the stability and reactivity toward ORR of the Pd NPs supported on doped and defective graphene. Some theoretical studies of Pd clusters supported on pristine, doped, and defective graphene for the ORR have been developed [12,13,27]. For example, Jia et al. performed DFT calculations to investigate the O2 adsorption on Pdn (n = 1–5) clusters supported on single vacancy graphene and proved that O2 adsorption on a graphene-supported Pd cluster induces a further O–O bond elongation [12]. Another first-principles study investigated the O, OH, and OOH adsorption on Pd13/defective graphene [27]; this work demonstrated that the defective graphene substrates stabilize the Pd NPs and reduce the adsorption energies of the species on Pd13/defective graphene compared to the Pd13 particles [27]. Additionally, the electronic structure of Pd13 clusters supported on defective graphene and their reactivity toward O adsorption have also been studied; it showed that the defective graphene could provide anchoring sites for these NPs by forming a strong metal–substrate interaction [13]. These theoretical studies of Pd NPs supported on graphene have provided great knowledge in the field of the Pt-free catalysts for the ORR [12,13,27]. However, it is essential to continue investigating on this topic in order to stablish new strategies about it. Based on the aforementioned statements, this study aims to investigate the ground-state structures and properties of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters, the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, defective graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and the reactivity of the Pdn, Pdn/C96H24, Pdn/C95H24:B, and Pdn/C95H24 (n = 8–10) systems toward oxygen adsorption.

The ground-state structures of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters were obtained from several dozen initial configurations. In this investigation, structures that had been previously reported by other authors as well as many other initial structures were determined using the Gupta Potentials (GP), which use a second-moment approximation of a tight-binding Hamiltonian, were considered [28]. The GMIN package was used to determinate the structure clusters, which generates the potential energy surface from the GP using a basin-hopping algorithm [29]. The Gupta parameters A(eV),
ζ
(eV), p, q, and r
0 (Å) used in the calculation of Pdn clusters were 0.1746, 1.718, 10.867, 3.742, and 2.7485, respectively [30]. GQDs are fragments of graphene having a size less than 20 nm and finite Bohr radius [31], which have been widely studied due to their exceptional properties such as optical, electrical and optoelectrical [32]. These GQDs have been widely used as graphene model in many DFT calculations for the interactions with water molecules, gas molecules, metal clusters, among others [33–36]. The di-circum-coronene (C96H24) structure was considered as model for the pristine GQDs. All initial structures were optimized by DFT calculations using the Orca package [37]. The functional revPBE was employed for all DFT calculations, which has a mean absolute error of 0.004 Ha for atomic total energies [38]. The Def2-SVP basis set was used to describe all electrons of the C, H, O, and B atoms [39], whereas the Pd atom was described using a quasi-relativistic effective core potential [40].

To investigate the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine, doped, and defective GQDs, the pristine (C96H24), B-doped (C95H24:B), and defective (C95H24) GQDs were used as models for the DFT calculations. The binding energy (Eb) of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on GQDs was calculated as follows:
(1)
E b
=
E
Pd n
/G
-
(E
Pd n
+
E G
)
where
E
Pd
n
/ G
is the total energy of the Pd clusters supported on GQDs,
E
G
is the energy of the GQDs, and
E
Pd
n
is the energy of the Pd cluster.

The oxygen adsorption energy (
E
ad
(
O
)
)
on the Pdn (n = 8–10) clusters (equation (2)) and on the Pdn clusters supported on GQDs (equation (3)) were calculated by the following equations:
(2)
E ad
(O) =
E
O/Pd n
-
(E O
+
E
Pd n
)
(3)
E ad
(O) =
E
O/Pd n
/G
-
(E O
+
E
Pd n
/G
)
where
E
O /
Pd
n
is the total energy of the cluster with the adsorbate (O),
E
O
is the energy of the oxygen atom,
E
Pd
n
is the energy of the Pd cluster,
E
O /
Pd
n
/ G
is the total energy of the clusters supported on GQDs with the O atom, and
E
Pd
n
/ G
is the energy of cluster supported on GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures and properties of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters
ce:section.ce:para: First, the ground-state structures of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters were investigated. The obtained ground-state structures of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters are illustrated in Fig. 1 . The ground-state structures of the Pdn clusters investigated in this study agree with those reported previously for Pdn (n = 3–7) [41–49], Pd8 [42,44,45,47–49], Pd9 [42,44,45,47,49], and Pd10 clusters [44,47,49]. According to the data referred, the results obtained in this work demonstrate the reliability of the computational method employed in this investigation.

In addition, the average bond lengths and binding energies per atom (BEA) were calculated. In Fig. 2 , the average bond lengths and BEA are presented. The average bond length from the Pd2 cluster to the Pd7 cluster tends to increase (see Fig. 2a)). For the Pd8 clusters, the average bond length decreases with respect to the Pd7 clusters, and this can be directly associated with the compact structure of the Pd8 cluster; furthermore, this tendency for the Pd8 cluster was previously reported [42,49]. Finally, from the Pd9 cluster to the Pd10 cluster, the average bond length decreases. In the BEA of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters (see Fig. 2b)), it can be observed that when increasing the cluster size, the energy tends to increase; this same trend has been reported in previous studies of palladium clusters [46,47]. Additionally, the calculated values are very similar to those reported previously [47,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, and defective GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the NPs on the support material is essential for the electrocatalysis of the ORR [14–16]. Thus, the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine, B-doped, and defective GQDs was investigated. The pristine (C96H24), B-doped (C95H24:B), and defective (C95H24) GQDs were used as models, and they are presented in Fig. 3 . The average C–C bond length for the GQDs is 1.42 Å, which is comparable to the previous DFT calculations and experimental results [50]. The adsorption sites of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters on the C96H24, C95H24:B, and C95H24 sheets are indicated with red crosses (see Fig. 3). The Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters were adsorbed in the same position on the C96H24, C95H24:B, and C95H24, to achieve a systematic analysis of the effects of the doping and defects on the stability of palladium clusters on GQDs. In the supporting information, the initial adsorption sites (without optimization) of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters on the GQDs sheets are presented.

First, the stability of the Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters on the C96H24 was investigated. The adsorption sites and Eb of the palladium clusters on the C96H24 are presented in Fig. 4 . The Eb of the palladium clusters on the C96H24 have an oscillatory behavior; the maximum value was found for the Pd3 cluster, whereas the lowest value was found for the Pd10 cluster. For all cases, the Eb calculated for the palladium clusters on the GQDs is less than 1 eV. This low Eb of the palladium clusters on the GQDs could be associated with the low coordination numbers between the palladium clusters and the GQDs because a low coordination number is related to low Eb of the clusters [51]. In this context, Lui and coworkers investigated the Eb on different sites of the pristine graphene with low coordination number between the Pd13 and pristine graphene, and they found Eb in a range from −0.20 to −1.07 eV [27].

The low reactivity of GQDs is well-known. Hence, to modify the reactivity of the GQDs, several approaches have been adopted such as the doping and addition of defects. The doping comprised replacing a carbon atom for a boron atom in the central part of the GQDs (see Fig. 3). Furthermore, the Pdn (n = 3–8) clusters were adsorbed on the C95H24:B. Finally, the stability of the Pdn (n = 3–8) clusters on the C95H24:B sheet was evaluated. The adsorption sites and Eb on the C95H24:B sheets are illustrated in Fig. 5 . The adsorption energies of the palladium clusters supported on C95H24:B sheets range from −0.79 to −1.73 eV. The highest adsorption energy was calculated for the Pd7 clusters, whereas the lower adsorption energy was calculated for the Pd10 cluster. It was demonstrated that the palladium clusters on the C95H24:B sheets are more stable than those on the C96H24 sheets because the boron atom acts as a strong charge accepting center in the graphene sheets [52]. This result is consistent with a previous study, in which it was found that the Eb of a Pd atom on a boron-doped graphene sheet significantly increased in comparison to that on the pristine graphene [52].

The doping proved to be an excellent strategy to modify the reactivity of the GQDs. An alternative approach to modify the GQDs that is attracting interest is the creation of defects on the GQDs. The defect in the GQDs is created by removing a carbon atom from the center of the sheet (see Fig. 3). In Fig. 6 , the adsorption sites and Eb of the palladium clusters on the C95H24 are presented. All the calculated Eb are around −5 eV, but it can be clearly observed that the Eb of Pd clusters on the defective GQDs increases significantly compared to the pristine and B-doped GQDs. Thus, the stability of the palladium clusters is enhanced on the defective GQDs. The results obtained in this study are consistent with those reported in the literature [53,54]. López and collaborators adsorbed Pdn clusters (n = 1–6) on a graphene vacancy and they found that Eb of Pd clusters is from −5.13 to −5.93 eV [53]. In another study, Pdn clusters (n = 1, 4, and 6) were supported on defective graphene, and the adsorption energies calculated were from −5.47 to −5.99 eV [54]. It is known that the defect modifies the graphene sheets because new states are formed near the Fermi level. Therefore, they can readily accept extra electrons [52]. These results suggest that the creation of defects is an effective strategy to improve the stability of the palladium clusters on the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen adsorption on the Pdn (n = 8–10) clusters/pristine, B-doped, and defective GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: A DFT based investigation was developed to study the ground-state structures and properties of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters, the stability of Pdn (n = 3–10) clusters supported on pristine (C96H24), B-doped (Pdn/C95H24:B), and defective (Pdn/C95H24) GQDs, and the reactivity of the Pdn, Pdn/C96H24, Pdn/C95H24:B, and Pdn/C95H24 (n = 8–10) systems toward oxygen adsorption. The calculated ground-state structures and properties for the Pdn (n = 8–10) clusters are consistent with those reported previously. The stability of palladium clusters supported on GQDs follows the trend: C95H24 > C95H24:B > C96H24. This demonstrates that defective GQDs is promising for its application as a support material for the ORR. In general, the reactivity of the palladium clusters is not significantly modified by being supported on the C96H24, C95H24:B, and C95H24 sheets. However, in various cases, the reactivity toward ORR (using an oxygen atom as a catalytic predictor) is slightly improved (e.g., Pd8/C96H24, Pd8/C95H24:B, Pd9/C95H24:B, and Pd10/C95H24 composites).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: F. Montejo-Alvaro thanks CONACYT for the doctoral scholarship. H. Cruz-Martínez thanks CONACYT for the doctoral and postdoctoral fellowship. The authors acknowledge the computational resource provided by the ABACUS-I supercomputer from CINVESTAV.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solidstatesciences.2019.04.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced electrocatalytic activity of cubic Pd nanoparticles towards the oxygen reduction reaction in acid media

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning metal cluster catalytic activity with morphology and composition: a DFT study of O2 dissociation at the global minimum of PtmPdn (m + n= 5) clusters

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of graphene‐based nanostructural materials for both catalyst supports and metal‐free catalysts in PEM fuel Cell oxygen reduction reactions
